title: Enhancing Depression Detection on Social Media with DSM-V Guided RAG and Fine-Tuned DeepSeek-R1_supp1-3699501.docx
authors: Shouliang Qi
publish: N/A
abstract: Depression significantly contributes to global disability, yet remains underdiagnosed due to lack of awareness. Social media platforms provide rich, real-time data reflecting users' emotional and psychological states, making them promising resources for automated depression detection. However, general-purpose Large Language Models (LLMs) often perform suboptimally in clinical domains, lacking interpretability and domain specificity. To address these limitations, this study introduces a knowledge-augmented framework combining Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) guided by clinical psychiatric criteria from the DSM-V with a fine-tuned DeepSeek-R1-7B model. We constructed a domain specific knowledge base comprising 329 expert-reviewed, retrieval-friendly DSM-V-derived entries. By retrieving relevant DSM-V-TR diagnostic criteria alongside user posts, our framework provides clinically grounded contextual information that may support human review by relating model predictions to established psychiatric criteria. Experiments on three balanced social media datasets showed that the DSM-V-guided RAG module, when combined with fine-tuned DeepSeek-R1-7B, achieved improved performance over the compared settings under the current experimental protocol. The retrieved DSM-V entries also provided clinically grounded contextual information that may help interpret model predictions. These findings suggest that DSM-V-guided knowledge augmentation is a promising direction for improving the transparency of benchmark-based depression detection on social media.

title: FlexPWL: A Flexible, Scalable, and Multiplier-Free Approach for Activation Functions on FPGA
authors: Ebrahim Fard, Janier Arias-Garcia, Hao Zhang, Seokbum Ko
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computers
abstract: The hardware implementation of nonlinear activation functions (AFs), such as the Sigmoid and Hyperbolic Tangent (Tanh), presents a significant bottleneck for deploying Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) on resource-constrained edge devices. Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs), as a leading platform for edge AI, face challenges in efficiently executing these functions due to their complex mathematical nature and limited computational resources. This paper proposes a novel, hardware-friendly piecewise linear (PWL) approximation method for implementing Sigmoid and Tanh functions. Our approach introduces new mathematical formulations for computing the y-intercept that significantly reduce Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Maximum Absolute Error (MXE) at no additional hardware cost. By consolidating the features of recent works, the proposed architecture eliminates a costly segment address encoder, supports pipelining for low-latency and high-frequency operation, and avoids the complexity and low precision of other methods. Moreover, it offers high configurability in bit width, number of segments, and input ranges, enabling adaptable deployment across diverse hardware and precision targets. A unified architecture is presented for both AFs, maintaining identical FPGA resource usage across functions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms prior state-of-the-art designs by up to 18.52× in accuracy, 437.29× in latency, and 3.71× in frequency, and achieves LUT and flip-flop savings of up to 11.63× and 12.42×, respectively.

title: HF-Head: A Lightweight Head Design for SAR Small-Ship Detection with Algorithm–Hardware Co-Optimization
authors: Zhenjiao Chen, Hepeng Hu, Wei Cheng, Binghe Ma, Feng Liang, Guilin Zhao
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) ship detection remains challenging in complex maritime scenes because small targets usually exhibit weak contours and limited textures and suffer from strong background interference, while edge deployment further demands low complexity and high efficiency. To address these challenges, this paper proposes HF-Head, a lightweight detection head for SAR small-ship detection with algorithm–hardware co-optimization. Built on the YOLO framework, HF-Head reconstructs the detection head with a unified depthwise separable design to improve deployment regularity and reduce computational cost, and further introduces a lightweight dual-branch enhancement mechanism, where a high-frequency branch strengthens edge and local-detail representation and a semantic branch enlarges the receptive field for contextual modeling.Aspatially gated injection strategy is used for adaptive feature fusion, and a re-parameterized folding scheme maintains stronger training-time representation while preserving single-path inference at deployment. Experiments on HRSID, SAR-Ship, and SSDD show that HF-Head reduces parameters by 13.2% and GFLOPs by 28.0% while maintaining or improving detection accuracy; specifically, mAP@0.5:0.95 is improved by 6.5%, 3.9%, and 3.4%, reaching 0.754, 0.554, and 0.661, respectively. FPGA RTL evaluation of the P3-level head under a near-iso-DSP budget further demonstrates a 3.81× latency speedup and a 58.5% reduction in energy per frame, reaching 240.66 mJ, indicating that HF-Head achieves a favorable accuracy–efficiency trade-off for edge-oriented SAR ship detection.

title: Dense Facial Landmark Detection in Thermal Infrared With Cross-Dataset Generalization Testing and Failure Detection
authors: Wiktoria Szumigraj, Marcin Kowalski
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Thermal infrared face analysis remains challenging compared to its visible spectrum counterpart due to reduced texture, sensor noise, and large variations across camera characteristics. These factors limit the generalization of existing landmark detectors, which exhibit substantial performance degradation when deployed on unseen datasets or acquisition conditions. In this study, we introduce a dense facial landmark detection framework that directly regresses a 478-point facial mesh from thermal images. The model is based on a ConvFormer-S36 backbone with a proposed lightweight Multi-Query Attention Pooling head, enabling the learning of stable thermal representations with reduced overfitting to dataset-specific statistics. Proposed model achieved stable vertical-axis regression behavior. We systematically analyzed failure modes across multiple architectures and identified distinct patterns that emerged under extreme poses, occlusions, or low-contrast conditions. We further proposed a lightweight, geometry-based failure detection mechanism that leverages the distinctive signature of errors to flag degraded predictions. We validated our framework by using several thermal infrared datasets for emotion recognition and landmarking. Quantitatively, our model achieved a normalized mean error of 0.045 on the external dataset after mapping the dense mesh to the 68-point standard format, whereas qualitative evaluations on external datasets demonstrated improved cross-dataset generalization under diverse imaging conditions, poses, and occlusions. Together, these results establish dense mesh-based thermal landmarking as a generalizable paradigm and highlight the importance of understanding the failure modes for deployment in real-world applications.

title: Collaborative Acceleration for Server-Side Multi-User View Synthesis_supp1-3694560.pdf
authors: Chubo Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: Real-time performance optimization is critical for interactive applications like Virtual Reality (VR). Existing methods primarily focus on single-user scenarios and often overlook spatial redundancy in multi-user environments. As collaborative experiences grow, delivering real-time rendering on lightweight devices becomes increasingly challenging. This paper proposes a spatial-similarity-based clustering method and a server-side multi-user multi-view rendering framework. By leveraging cross-user redundancy in reference views, the framework reuses intermediate transformation results to improve computational efficiency. Server-side rendering further enhances performance on lightweight clients. Experiments on NVIDIA A100 GPU show that our method reduces computational workload by 23.68%-24.10% and H2D memory copy overhead by 66.81% compared to traditional DIBR. Compared with VINet and IBRNet, our approach achieves higher efficiency in computation, memory, and runtime, enabling real-time synthesis of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4096 \times 2048$</tex-math></inline-formula> images within 16.63 ms (approximately 60 FPS). Project code and scripts: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/yuanaiya/CASSMVS</uri>.

title: A High-Speed Omnidirectional Ceiling Mobile Robot Using a Synchronized Crawler-Hanging Mechanism_supp1-3699154.mp4
authors: Hiroto Hasegawa
publish: N/A
abstract: We present a ceiling mobile robot capable of high-speed transport with versatile routing. Based on the crawler-hanging mechanism proposed in previous work, the robot introduces several key improvements. First, we enhanced hanging stability during high-speed travel by synchronizing the ceiling-hanging motion with the crawler's rotation via guide mechanisms. This approach achieved reliable operation at 1.35 m/s, a 35% improvement over earlier designs. Additionally, a position correction method utilizing claw insertion into ceiling holes effectively corrected <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula>2 mm positional errors per hanging action, ensuring accurate alignment during travel. Second, we developed a direction-changing mechanism for static ceiling panels by relocating rolling mechanisms from turntables to the robot itself. This design enabled turning at 45 deg/s with a 60 kg payload and sideway movement at 0.2 m/s, enhancing routing versatility. These advancements improve ceiling robot mobility and expand the applicability of ceiling transport systems for industrial and logistics environments.

title: Robust Intra-Pulse Frequency Coding Against Time-Gated Frequency-Following Jamming via Belief-State Reinforcement Learning_supp1-3699318.pdf
authors: Zhenhua Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Constrained by instantaneous receiver bandwidth and intermittent jamming mechanisms, radar anti-jamming against time-gated frequency-following jammers inherently degrades into a time-frequency dual-domain Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP). To overcome these physical constraints, this paper proposes an end-to-end belief-state reinforcement learning framework for robust intra-pulse frequency coding. By directly mapping short-time Fourier transform (STFT) sequences to agility commands, the architecture eliminates feature truncation errors. Furthermore, a state-transition-aware reward mechanism is designed to establish a phase transition boundary, analytically resolving the exploration-exploitation dilemma under partial observability. Feature manifold analysis reveals that the agent dynamically reconstructs the jammer's generative mechanism rather than memorizing static sequences. Simulations demonstrate the method achieves near-optimal steady-state robustness and enables rapid policy re-convergence against non-stationary strategy shifts without offline retraining.

title: Rotatable Continuous Aperture Array (CAPA)-based Multi-user MIMO Communications
authors: Mengyu Qian, Xidong Mu, Li You, Michail Matthaiou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: This paper proposes a rotatable continuous-aperture array (R-CAPA)-based multi-user multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) architecture. Aiming at maximizing the system spectral efficiency (SE), an integral-based optimizing problem is formulated for the joint optimization of the CAPA beamformers at the base station (BS) and the boresight direction of each CAPA at each user. To address this problem, we develop an alternating optimization (AO)-based algorithm. For the BS-CAPA beamformer optimization subproblem, we employ the calculus of variation (CoV) theory to obtain the optimized beamformer. For the user CAPA boresight direction optimization subproblem, we leverage the gradient-ascent method to derive the optimized boresight direction of each R-CAPA with the current beamformers. Our simulation results show that, compared to conventional static CAPAs, R-CAPAs can achieve significantly higher system SE with enhanced degrees of freedom.

title: NPLNet: A Noise-Prior Lightweight Network for On-Device Acoustic Fault Diagnosis of Railway Point Machines from Noisy Measurements
authors: Junxiang Yao, Xiaoxi Hu, Chengzhi Jiang, Yuhan Huang, Huan Wang, Zhuyun Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Despite the remarkable success of deep learning in fault diagnosis for Railway Point Machines (RPMs), existing models often overlook the non-ideal conditions inherent in manual field maintenance, such as environmental noise and signal instabilities stemming from handheld acquisition. This leaves a critical gap: achieving high-performance diagnosis while maintaining the lightweight design required for end-to-end deployment on handheld diagnostic instruments remains a formidable challenge. We address this gap by proposing the Noise-Prior Lightweight Network (NPLNet), an end-to-end lightweight fault diagnosis network. To improve robustness to amplitude fluctuations, we introduce the Random Amplitude Perturbation (RAP) strategy, an empirical augmentation designed to mimic gain variations commonly observed in imperfect handheld acquisition. The architecture features a backbone that integrates a Noise-Prior Spectral Filtering (NPSF) module for robust noise suppression and stacked Attentive Hierarchical Redundancy-reduction Cell (AHRC) units for multi-scale feature extraction. This is followed by a Multi-granularity Semantic Aggregation (MSA) head that performs robust classification by fusing semantic information from the hierarchical feature maps. Experimental results on the noisy ZDJ9 RPM acoustic dataset demonstrate that NPLNet achieves a peak diagnostic accuracy of 99.59% with a high inference throughput of 228.9 samples/s on a single-thread ARM Cortex-A53 CPU. This performance demonstrates a favorable trade-off along the accuracy-throughput Pareto frontier, outperforming several lightweight architectures in accuracy while maintaining competitive CPU throughput. The proposed network provides a lightweight and noise-prior-guided solution for handheld RPM acoustic diagnosis in noisy environments.

title: Bayesian Inference-Based Model-Free Predictive Current Control for SPMSM Drives
authors: Lu Liu, Rongbo Lai, Shihong Ding, Li Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: To address the issue of control gain dependency on the accuracy of stator inductance in model-free predictive current control (MFPCC) for surface-mounted permanent magnet synchronous motors (SPMSM), this article proposes a novel approach integrating Bayesian inference for online parameter identification. The method combines an ultra-local model with an extended state observer (ESO) and incorporates a Metropolis-Hastings sampling algorithm to continuously estimate the posterior distribution of the inductance parameter, enabling real-time self-tuning of the controller. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the method achieves high-precision inductance identification, mitigating performance degradation caused by parameter variations and enhancing the robustness of the drive system.

title: Adaptive Downsampling and Refinement for Fast Iterative Closest Point
authors: Neha Bhatt, Zhaodong Zhou, Krunal Mehta, Jun Chen
publish: IEEE Systems, Man, and Cybernetics Letters
abstract: Iterative closest point (ICP) and its variants rep resent a fundamental technique for rigid registration between two point sets, with wide applications ranging from robotics to 3D reconstruction. The primary limitations of ICP include slow convergence due to its linear convergence rate, along with the per-iteration nearest-neighbor search, which scales linearly with source cloud size and dominates runtime on dense datasets. To address these issues, this paper proposes a modified ICP method that achieves fast convergence while maintaining robust alignment. Specifically, we introduce an adaptive threshold-based downsampling technique that selectively reduces the number of source points processed during early iterations, decreasing the computational cost for each iteration. This is followed by a full cloud refinement stage designed to recover alignment accuracy and mitigate any degradation introduced by the downsampling phase. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves notable reductions in computational time across the majority of benchmark datasets, including Stanford mesh models and LiDAR point clouds, while maintaining comparable registration accuracy relative to the baseline method.

title: Deciphering Object Concepts: Hierarchical Cross-Modal Relational Reasoning for Mining Object-Attribute-Affordance Associations
authors: Yuxuan Wang, Muli Yang, Yihang Zhu, Jiexi Yan, Fen Fang, Xulei Yang, Cheng Deng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Object Concept Learning (OCL) aims to recognize high-level attributes and affordances of objects and to infer the causal relationships between them. The key is to accurately model the many-to-many mapping between objects and concepts: While an object may possess multiple concepts, a concept can also belong to multiple objects. Existing methods primarily rely on attention mechanisms to capture label correlations, which limits their ability to comprehend high-level concepts and to perform effective causal reasoning. Inspired by the human cognitive process of progressive understanding, a Hierarchical Cross-Modal Relational Reasoning (CORE) framework is proposed to enhance the understanding of object concepts through hierarchical interaction and reasoning between visual and textual modalities. Specifically, a coarse-to-fine relational reasoning module is developed, in which multi-step learnable prompts are employed to progressively localize the conceptual information of objects, thereby improving the accuracy of object-concept mapping. Subsequently, to facilitate the modeling of causal relationships between object attributes and affordances, a counterfactual reasoning mechanism is introduced. By constructing counterfactual samples and distinguishing the predictive outputs of factual and counterfactual parts, the model's ability to capture causality among concepts is enhanced. Significant performance gains and extensive visualization analysis demonstrate the superiority of our method.

title: Robustness of SAM: Segment Anything under Corruptions and beyond_supp1-3698837.pdf
authors: Yu Qiao
publish: N/A
abstract: Segment anything model (SAM) is notable for its ability to cut out any object and its impressive zero-shot transfer performance with prompt guidance. However, its robustness under corruption remains underexplored. Prior work indicates that SAM is biased towards texture rather than shape. We first evaluate its robustness against style transfer (synthetic corruption), followed by 15 common corruptions, and find that SAM demonstrates robustness in these scenarios. Moreover, we assess SAM's resilience to local patch occlusion and adversarial patch attacks, finding it resilient to occlusion but vulnerable to patch attacks. We observe that patch attacks disrupt the feature map and misguide the attention mechanism, whereas patch occlusion does not cause such misguidance. Given that visible patch attacks are easily detectable, we further investigate robustness against global adversarial attacks that remain imperceptible to humans by employing basic attacks and a new mask-aware loss. Our findings suggest that SAM's vulnerability primarily stems from its more reliance on high-frequency signals compared to low-frequency ones. Moreover, we further investigate the targeted transferability of adversarial examples and propose a contrastive learning-driven approach with a plug-and-play modulation module, enabling more effective transferability across different SAM models. In addition, we investigate a range of defense strategies to enhance the robustness of SAM, as well as stronger and more adaptive attack methods. Overall, this work provides a comprehensive study of SAM's robustness across diverse scenarios and is expected to offer valuable insights into its practical applicability and effectiveness in real-world settings.

title: An Edge-Sensing Measurement Framework for Cross-Sensor Urban Altitude Estimation via Physics-Informed Neural Fields
authors: Zichun Chen, Siyu Zhu, Shuo Sun, Yanxing Wu, Yongxing Wang, Xiao Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Very low-level (VLL) airspace and Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) operations require different aircraft, infrastructure systems, terrain databases, and traffic-management services to interpret altitude in a consistent manner. Unlike horizontal position, where latitude and longitude provide a broadly adopted defacto reference, the vertical channel remains fragmented. Land surveying products commonly use mean-sea-level or orthometric height, aviation systems rely on barometric pressure altitude, and many UAV applications use GNSS height above ellipsoid or height above ground level. This lack of a unified height system creates ambiguity when heterogeneous legacy and emerging systems coexist in dense urban airspace. This paper addresses this vertical harmonization problem by constructing a continuous conversion field that transforms barometric measurements into a unified geometric height reference. To this end, we develop an edge-sensing measurement framework that combines a custom GeoBox multi-sensor IoT terminal with a physics-informed neural field (PINF). The proposed PINF embeds the hypsometric equation, multi-resolution hash encoding, a physics-derived pressure-bias feature, and altitude-aware curriculum learning to decouple sensor-specific barometer bias from environmental correction. The framework is evaluated on an urban GeoBox deployment using an 8-fold leave-one-sensor-out protocol, where each heldout GeoBox is excluded from training and validation. The proposed method achieves a mean absolute error of 3.55 m, corresponding to a 90.4% reduction relative to the uncalibrated physics baseline. These results demonstrate the potential of edge-sensed, physically constrained neural fields for building local height-conversion infrastructure for low altitude applications.

title: Hybrid Relay Architecture: A Decentralized and Semantically-agnostic Interoperability Framework for Blockchain
authors: Liyuan Ma, Sheng Chen, Yiran Lv, Boyue Luan, Xiulong Liu, Keqiu Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computers
abstract: The rapidly expanding blockchain ecosystem faces a critical interoperability crisis—evidenced by siloed total value locked and bridge losses. Existing interoperability solutions are trapped in a paradigm dilemma: centralized relays offer performance but introduce single points of failure, while decentralized relay-chains provide security but suffer from poor scalability and rigidity. To overcome these limitations, we introduce the Hybrid Relay Architecture, a new decentralized interoperability paradigm that reconciles the efficiency of centralized relays with the security guarantees of decentralized systems via dynamic role stratification and probabilistic trust coordination. We instantiate this paradigm as Celestial, which realizes hybrid relaying through (1) a unified interchain data unit for coordination, (2) a geo-aware topology that enables adaptive role assignment, and (3) a proof of cross-chain transmission protocol that incentivizes honest node participation. Implemented in 12K+ LoC and evaluated across 90+ nodes spanning three continents, Celestial achieves 3,340 TPS with sub-600ms P99 latency, recovers from faults 6.4× faster than state-of-the-art systems, and reduces verification cost by 63% under adaptive attacks.

title: Robust Open-Set Signal Emitter Identification Using Collaborative Learning and Distributed Attention Networks
authors: Liangtian Wan, Yixuan Ya, Lu Sun, Jiashuai Wang, Kuixian Li, Yun Lin, Xianpeng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Signal emitter identification (SEI) has significant practical value in both military and civilian domains. In the military domain, it is used for target tracking and identification, while in civilian applications, it plays a crucial role in spectrum management and radio security. Current SEI technologies mainly focus on closed-set classification. However, in real-life scenarios, the electromagnetic environment is usually open and nonstatic. Existing algorithms suffer significant performance degradation when confronted with a large number of unknown class data. In the context of cyber–physical–social systems, SEI is required to break through the traditional single-node analysis model. Therefore, this article proposes an open-set recognition method based on collaborative learning of knowledge and data and distributed attention networks (CoDiNet). This method achieves robust recognition in complex electromagnetic environments through three-stage optimization involving collaborative learning of knowledge and data, distributed residual attention networks, and socialized dynamic decision-making. To validate the proposed methodology, empirical evaluations were performed on the ADS-B dataset, demonstrating that the CoDiNet framework achieves effective discrimination of both registered and emergent emitter categories under open-set conditions. The false alarm rate is significantly reduced, and the risk of misclassifying known classes as unknown classes is lowered. The method achieves a more balanced and stable performance, while also enhancing the robustness and reliability of the system. Overall, CoDiNet outperforms existing algorithms.

title: An Angularly Stable and Bidirectional Frequency Selective Rasorber Based on an Antenna-Filter-Antenna Architecture
authors: Peiqi Liu, Xiangkun Kong, Yukun Zou, Ruotong Guo, Chenrui Jiang, Lingqi Kong, Qiang Chen, Hui Pan
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Frequency selective rasorbers (FSRs) have been widely investigated for their capability to reduce radar cross-section (RCS) by integrating frequency selectivity with electromagnetic absorption. However, existing three-dimensional FSRs often suffer from performance degradation under large oblique incidence angles due to the angular sensitivity of their resonant structures. To address this challenge, this paper presents an angularly stable and bidirectional FSR based on a novel antenna-filter-antenna (A-F-A) architecture. The proposed design features a symmetric receiving antenna–absorptive coupled-line filter–transmitting antenna signal processing chain. By converting incident spatial waves into guided waves via slot antennas, the filtering and absorption responses are effectively decoupled from the incidence angle, thereby achieving inherent angular stability. Furthermore, the structural symmetry of the A-F-A configuration endows the rasorber with bidirectional performance, ensuring identical scattering characteristics for waves incident from both forward and backward directions. Experimental results demonstrate that at a 0° incident angle for TM polarization, the proposed FSR achieves an actual measured insertion loss of 0.81 dB at 9.58 GHz and an absorption rate exceeding 80% across the 11.16–14.00 GHz band. Most notably, the device exhibits remarkable stability under oblique incidence, maintaining its transmission and absorption performance up to 50° for TM polarization. This work provides a robust solution for realizing high-performance radomes and stealth platforms requiring wide-angle and bidirectional electromagnetic wave control.

title: Environmental Adaptation Enabled by an Amplitude-Tunable Traveling Wave Robot with a Soft Corkscrew (ATWBot)_supp1-3697245.mp4

title: A Comprehensive General Model of Tendon-Actuated Concentric Tube Robots with Multiple Tubes and Tendons_supp1-3699204.mp4
authors: Pejman Kheradmand
publish: N/A
abstract: Tendon-actuated concentric tube mechanisms combine the advantages of tendon-driven continuum robots and concentric tube robots while addressing their respective limitations. They overcome the restricted degrees of freedom often seen in tendon-driven designs and mitigate issues such as restricted curvatures associated with concentric tube robots. However, a complete and general mechanical model for these systems remains an open problem. In this work, we propose a Cosserat rod-based framework for modeling the general case of n concentric tubes, each actuated by mi tendons, where i = {1, . . . , n}. The model allows each tube to twist and elongate while enforcing a shared centerline for bending. We validate the proposed framework through experiments with two-tube and three-tube assemblies under various tendon routing configurations, achieving tip prediction errors < 4% of the robot’s total length. We further demonstrate the model’s generality by applying it to existing robots in the field, where maximum tip deviations remain around 5% of the total length. This model provides a foundation for accurate shape estimation and control of advanced tendon-actuated concentric tube robots.

title: Minimum Data Rate for Consensus of Linear MASs Under Weighted-Average Protocols
authors: Can Zhao, Liwei An, Lili Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: This article investigates the minimum data rate for achieving consensus of discrete-time linear multiagent systems under weighted-average protocols. The agents transmit information to each other over a network under data rate constraints. The minimum data rate required to enable the multiagent system to achieve consensus is obtained. It is shown that the minimum data rate is an intrinsic property of the multiagent system, which is independent of encoding–decoding schemes, controller gains, and communication topologies (as long as they are connected) chosen. Accordingly, an encoding–decoding scheme is proposed which achieves the minimum data rate. Then, it is proved that the multiagent system can achieve consensus based on the proposed scheme. Further, the minimum data rate for achieving asymptotic consensus with a given asymptotic convergence rate is derived. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed scheme is verified by simulation examples.

title: Smart Vending Machine For Grocery Shop with Custom Quantity Dispensing
authors: P.Ranjith Kumar, M.Vimala, S.Ramasamy, K.Kamalesh, M.Kannuppandi, B.Madhankumar
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: The creation of an automated vending machine system with an Arduino controller for effective and user-friendly operation is presented in this project. Because the system is made to take user input via a keypad, a variety of goods, including rice, sugar, and other groceries. The machine automatically dispenses the appropriate amount of the selected item based on the user's selection. Servo motors, which are controlled by the Arduino to precisely drop the required quantity of each item, are used to manage the process. By reducing human intervention, increasing transaction speed, and guaranteeing accurate dispensing, the system seeks to optimize vending operations. Furthermore, the solution incorporates effective real-time control, guaranteeing a smooth and dependable vending process. This automated vending machine shows how Arduino-based solutions can be used in common consumer applications, offering accuracy, convenience, and dependability in a form that is simple to implement. All things considered, the developed vending machine not only streamlines the dispensing process but also encourages sustainability by cutting down on packaging waste and allowing customers to buy just the amounts they require. This demonstrates the potential of Arduino-based smart vending systems in influencing the direction of automated retail and makes it a workable and scalable solution for both urban supermarkets and rural retail settings.

title: Bias and Fairness in AI Systems: Theoretical Analysis and Empirical Validation Through Reweighing Mitigation
authors: Louis Adamu Obadiah, Daniel Godwin, Ndachimya Magaji Edward, Nibras Mohammad Abubakar, Jibrailu Daniel, Douglas Kilyobas Titus
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence increasingly influences highstakes decisions in employment, lending, healthcare, and criminal justice. While AI systems promise efficiency and objectivity, they risk institutionalizing discrimination at scale when trained on biased data or designed without explicit fairness constraints. This paper presents a comprehensive investigation bridging theoretical analysis with empirical validation to address bias and fairness in AI. A systematic analysis of bias sources and fairness frameworks is integrated with a Python-based fairness assessment framework implemented using the AI Fairness 360 (AIF360) toolkit. Using the UCI Adult Income dataset, Logistic Regression is applied as the baseline classifier, and Reweighing is implemented as a preprocessing mitigation technique to evaluate algorithmic fairness across gender groups. Empirical results demonstrate measurable gender bias in the baseline model (Statistical Parity Difference: −0.175; Equal Opportunity Difference: −0.258). Following Reweighing mitigation, fairness disparities were substantially reduced (SPD: −0.062; EOD: +0.060), representing improvements of 64.6 % and 76.7 % respectively. These fairness gains were achieved with only a modest accuracy trade-off, as model accuracy declined from 0.825 to 0.812 (−1.3 percentage points). ROC analysis further confirmed convergence in discriminative performance across gender groups, with the gender AUC gap reducing from 0.054 to −0.014 following mitigation, indicating a reversal from male to marginal female discriminative advantage post-mitigation. An extended multi-classifier evaluation further demonstrates that Reweighing's effectiveness varies substantially across algorithm types achieving strong SPD reductions in Logistic Regression (64.5 %) and SVM (63.5 %), while showing limited impact on ensemble methods such as Random Forest, suggesting that mitigation effectiveness is algorithm-dependent. These findings provide quantitative evidence that algorithmic bias is both detectable and meaningfully mitigable through systematic fairness interventions, while demonstrating that fairness improvements need not require severe degradation in predictive performance.

title: Software Sustainability Debt and the Governance-Process-Execution Framework: Evidence from 513,000 Real-World CI/CD Workflows
authors: Rohit Dhawan, Minav Suresh Patel, Priyank Desai, Ankush Dhar
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: Software systems increasingly drive computing's carbon footprint, yet most workloads execute without sustainability governance. This study introduces Software Sustainability Debt (SSD) as the avoidable emissions accumulating from unmanaged execution, and the Governance-Process-Execution (GPE) framework as an enforceable solution through explicit policy guardrails. Analyzing 513,000 GitHub Actions workflows across three European grids under operationally constrained parameters, we demonstrate that governed approaches achieve 1% single-region reductions while maintaining SLA compliance, scaling to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0. 7 \%}$</tex> (GB-DE), <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 9. 9 \%}$</tex> (GB-FR), <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 0. 6 \%}$</tex> (DEFR), and 32.4% (tri-region) through cross-regional coordination. Unlike prior grid-aware systems, which act opportunistically when conditions are favorable, GPE enforces sustainability with the same operational guarantees as cost, SLA, and latency, making it deployable today rather than hypothetical. These results demonstrate that sustainability governance can operate under strict operational constraints, providing organizations a deployable framework for carbon accountability that maintains SLA compliance while enabling material emission reductions where infrastructure flexibility permits.

title: Machine Learning for Predicting and Managing Industrial Co and Nox Emissions: Data-Driven Modeling for Sustainable Production
authors: Dilara Abzhanova, Sapar Toxanov, Andrii Biloshchytskyi
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: Industrial emissions of nitrogen oxides (NOx) and carbon monoxide (CO) pose significant risks to human health and climate. Conventional monitoring techniques do not enable early prediction of regulatory exceedances. This work suggests a strategy for predicting CO and NOx emissions utilizing a gas turbine unit's digital twin architecture and machine learning algorithms. Four regression models - linear, polynomial (second degree), Ridge, and Lasso - were used and contrasted. The models were trained using historical data from an industrial facility (more than 35,000 records for the years 2020–2025), including key technological parameters such as ambient conditions, compressor discharge pressure (CDP), and turbine inlet temperature (TIT). The test results showed that the polynomial model was the most accurate: the coefficient of determination R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> climbed to 0.78, and the mean squared error (MSE) decreased by 22%. Sensitivity analysis revealed that CO emissions are positively impacted by compressor discharge pressure (CDP) and negatively impacted by turbine inlet temperature (TIT). Unlike existing studies based on simulated or standalone models, this work demonstrates a closed-loop digital twin prototype validated on real industrial sensor data with integrated diagnostics and sensitivity analysis. The proposed approach improves the accuracy of environmental performance assessment and can be used in proactive environmental management systems, ESG reporting, and sustainable industrial operations.

title: Artificial Intelligence based Object Detection Assistant for the Visually Impaired
authors: K. Sasirekha, K. Shanthini, M. Divyaraj
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: This paper discusses Vision Aid, a real-time, multimodal artificial intelligence system intended to provide visually impaired persons with greater awareness of their environment and the ability to move independently. The proposed system combines five complementary perception modules: YOLOv8, used to detect objects; MiDaS, used to estimate depth; EasyOCR, used to read text; MediaPipe, used to recognize gestures; and a Vision Transformer, used to analyze facial expressions. Unlike traditional assistive systems, which have isolated modules, the proposed system integrates these modules to enable holistic analysis. A centralized fusion system ranks safety-critical objects based on the depth-based ranking and contextual decision rule. To achieve minimal latency, the system manages live video streams and provides structural auditory feedback with the help of an asynchronous text-to-speech module. End-to-end and benchmark testing on real-world and experimental data show high performance at the module level, with 89.4% mAP, 91.2% accuracy, and an end-to-end task success rate of 88.6%. The framework had an average speed of 31.7 FPS and an average response latency of 0.7 seconds. The user assessment with visually impaired participants showed clear, responsive, and lower cognitive load. The findings substantiate the hypothesis that the proposed multimodal fusion architecture will deliver credible real-time support, improved situational awareness, and increased safety in a dynamic environment.

title: Smart Solar Advisor: AI-Driven IoT-Based System for Location-Aware Monthly Energy Forecasting
authors: Chamika Dilshan, Limasha Madusarani, Vihanga Upamal, Malmi Nadee, Thilini Jayalath, Nushkan Nismi
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Recent advances in Internet of Things technologies and machine learning, including real-time monitoring of environmental conditions, have greatly improved the accuracy of renewable energy forecasting via data-supported predictions. This paper introduces the Smart Solar Advisor, an IoT and site-specific based assessment framework for estimating the expected monthly energy production of solar photovoltaic systems developed prior to their installation. A sensor-based module collects environmental measurements at the site level via the Internet to a central server. The server processes input data to perform validation, normalization of data and anomaly detection to achieve consistent and reliable input data. These processed input data are then supplied to a trained regression model using the XGBoost algorithm deployed as a serialized artefact, which outputs an accurate estimation of the expected monthly energy generation of the solar photovoltaic system. Furthermore, the use of nearby site similarities enables more reliable predictions in areas that do not have enough historical data by referencing locations with similar geography. The framework serves as a practical, accurate decision-support tool to assist users in assessing solar potential, supporting sustainable energy use while making informed decisions on where to invest.

title: A Fairness-Aware Constraint Satisfaction Framework for University Exam Timetabling with Dual-Role Instructor Conflict Modeling
authors: Jibrailu Daniel, Yusuf Garga Bello, Emin Çağan Apaydın, Atakan Onay, Mutaz Wael, Nevşin Nağaş, Sudenur Yanık
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Exam scheduling at universities is an NP-hard problem due to high levels of constraint interaction. Existing Constraint Programming (CP) approaches to the University Exam Timetabling Problem (UETP) treat the instructor pool and the student body as mutually exclusive sets, omit room assignment from the constraint model, and address workload balance only as a soft post-processing heuristic rather than as a first-class optimisation objective. This paper closes all three gaps within a single, unified Constraint Satisfaction Optimisation Problem (CSOP) framework. The three principal novelties over prior CP-based work are: (i) a formally encoded dualrole instructor conflict hard constraint (H4) that prevents PhD candidates from being simultaneously examined and assigned as invigilators, a constraint class absent from all reviewed CP formulations; (ii) a multi-room Boolean assignment matrix that allows large-enrollment examinations to span multiple physical venues within the CP model itself, rather than being resolved by a post-processing heuristic; and (iii) a min-max workload fairness proxy integrated as a primary weighted CP-SAT objective, providing a provably tighter per-instructor bound than the variance minimisation objectives used in prior work. The model is solved by the Google OR-Tools CP-SAT solver. Experiments on four instances of the Carter/Toronto benchmark suite and on real institutional data from a metropolitan university demonstrate that all hard constraints are satisfied across every tested instance, near-optimal workload fairness (min-max gap 0–3) is achieved on three of four benchmarks, and the monolithic formulation scales to 189 examinations within practical time limits. A structural scalability boundary is identified at approximately 300 examinations for the monolithic formulation, beyond which column generation decomposition is required. An ablation study exposes a fundamental tradeoff between consecutive-invigilation avoidance and long-term fairness.

title: A Scalable OCR System to Bridge the Annotation Gap in Low Resource Languages
authors: Ashlin Deepa R N, R Lakshmi Sravani, Arra Deepthi
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Optical Character Recognition (OCR) has evolved significantly over the years and has achieved strong performance for languages such as English using traditional machine learning and deep learning techniques. However, OCR for Indian languages remain a challenging task due to the complexity of scripts and large variations in handwriting styles. Although research has been carried out in this domain, achieving real-time OCR performance for Indian languages is still limited. Most machine learning, deep learning, semi-supervised, and self-supervised approaches require a large amount of labeled or unlabeled data, which is scarce for Indian languages. This data scarcity becomes a major bottleneck in developing robust OCR systems for Indian scripts. To address this limitation, this paper focuses on analyzing Few-shot learning which is particularly suitable for low-resource scenarios. This technique aims to achieve accurate recognition using a pretrained model and a support set. The training is limited to a small set of labelled samples. The research shows that Few shots approach outperforms many State-of-the-art techniques under supervised learning. Here, the applicability of few-shot learning techniques for handwritten OCR in Indian languages is explored in detail.

title: Hardware Acceleration of User-Based Collaborative Filtering on an FPGA-Based SoC
authors: Burak Batmaz, Zeynep Batmaz
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: User-based collaborative filtering is effective in recommender systems, but its similarity computation stage is computationally expensive for embedded platforms. This paper presents a hardware-accelerated user-based collaborative filtering implementation on the AMD Xilinx Kria KV260 FPGA-based embedded SoC. The proposed design accelerates cosine similarity computation using a memory-centric architecture with optimized AXI interfaces for efficient data transfer. The accelerator is implemented using Vitis High-Level Synthesis and integrated with a C++ host application through the Xilinx Runtime framework. Experimental results on the MovieLens <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 M}$</tex> dataset show that the proposed approach preserves prediction accuracy while achieving up to approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times$</tex> speedup over a single-core software implementation. These results show the potential of FPGA-based acceleration for low-latency recommender systems in embedded environments.

title: Leveraging Harmonic Analysis for Feature Extraction and Selection in Time-Series Data Processing
authors: Yong Wang, Qiongqiong Yao, Xiaowen Chen, Jinghao Hei, Bing Liu, Jinke Wang
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Traditional time-domain analysis and conventional frequency-domain deep learning architectures often lack adaptive frequency-domain feature selection mechanisms, easily leading to severe parameter redundancy, feature aliasing, and overfitting in low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) environments. To address this issue, this study proposes a comprehensive algorithm framework for time series data feature extraction and selection based on harmonic analysis (TF-HAS). This framework innovatively introduces the theory of diagonal bidirectional integral bispectrum (DBIB) and harmonic aggregation entropy (HaAgEn) based on higher-order statistics, effectively isolating and removing Gaussian background noise from the physical level using a quadratic phase coupling mechanism. Based on this, a spectral feature sparse selection mechanism integrating frequency-domain self-attention and Top-K masking is designed to actively block the backpropagation of high-frequency redundant noise. Furthermore, the top layer of the model combines contrastive time-frequency representation enhancement techniques with a KolmogorovArnold network (KAN) using B-spline functions, significantly improving the model's decision resolution and generalization performance under complex mappings. Experimental results based on the Mendeley power quality disturbance dataset, real sea trial axis-frequency electromagnetic field data, and long-sequence prediction benchmark datasets (ETTh1, Weather) demonstrate that the TF-HAS framework not only exhibits excellent error suppression capabilities in long-range prediction tasks but also achieves a classification accuracy of over 96.8% in extremely low signal-to-noise ratio environments. This study provides effective theoretical basis and technical reference for feature purification, dimensionality reduction selection, and lightweight modeling of complex non-stationary time series.

title:
ML-FS
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    -CDA- Meta-Learning-Driven Few-Shot Skin Lesion Segmentation with Cross-Dataset Adaptation
authors: M. Megala, T.R. Nisha Dayana
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Skin lesion segmentation is one of the most critical tasks for the early detection of melanoma; however, the performance of segmentation is often limited by the lack of annotated data and considerable domain variability among dermoscopic data available in various datasets e.g., International Skin Imaging Collaboration archive, PH2 Dataset and HAM10000. Conventional deep learning models are trained by feeding them with large labeled datasets and are not generalizable to imaging devices and acquisition settings. This paper proposes a framework for the Meta-Learning-Driven Few-Shot Skin Lesion Segmentation with Cross-Dataset Adaptation (ML-FS2-CDA) to solve these problems. The framework incorporates episodic meta-learning, prototype-guided attention segmentation, style-content feature disentanglement, domain adversarial alignment and self-supervised consistency regularization. Experimental evaluation with three public datasets shows the superior performance including Dice scores of 92.5% on ISIC 2018, 94.0% on PH2 and 96.5% on HAM10000, resulting in better performance than several state-of-the-art methods under limited supervision. The results confirm a greater cross domain robustness and a lower point of dependency for the annotations. The proposed approach provides a scalable and data efficient solution for the skin lesions segmentation in heterogeneity environments to be clinically reliable.
